title: Chicken souvlaki
subtitle: Skewered chicken thigh marinated in lemon, oregano, and olive oil.
excerpt: Greek skewered chicken: thigh cut into chunks, marinated in lemon, garlic, oregano, and olive oil for two hours, then grilled hot until the edges char and the centres stay juicy. Served on warm pitta with tzatziki, sliced tomato, and red onion.
sourceNotes: Souvlaki (from the Greek for 'small skewer') is the Greek take on a cooking method that goes back to antiquity; the dish in something close to its modern form appears in Greek cookery records from the early twentieth century. The marinade is the variable that distinguishes regional and family versions: lemon and oregano are the constants; garlic and bay vary. Pork is the traditional meat across most of the mainland, but chicken-thigh souvlaki has become the more common version in Greek tavernas and outside Greece. Served on a skewer with rice, in a pitta bread as a wrap (souvlaki pita), or on a plate of meze.

recipe.freezeNotes: Marinated raw chicken freezes well in the marinade for up to one month. Defrost overnight; the marinade continues working through the defrost.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly for a barbecue. Marinate in zip-top bags so the meat sits in the marinade rather than floating on the surface.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate up to twenty-four hours in advance. Beyond that the lemon begins to denature the protein and the texture turns mealy.

content.text: Boneless chicken thighs, not breasts. The dark meat takes a hot grill better and forgives a minute too long on the heat; breast goes dry. Cut the meat into pieces large enough to char on the outside without overcooking the centre, around 3 cm cubes. The marinade is lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, and oregano in plain proportions: half a lemon's worth of juice for every two thighs, two tablespoons of oil, a clove of garlic, a teaspoon of dried oregano.

Marinate for two hours at least, four is better, twenty-four is the absolute limit. Beyond that the lemon acid turns the protein chalky and you have lost the texture before the heat ever touched it. Skewers can be wood (soaked thirty minutes so they do not char) or metal. The cooking is fast and hot: a screaming hot griddle pan, or a barbecue once the coals are white. Two minutes a face, four faces, twelve minutes total.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, around 1 kg total, cut into 3 cm cubes
1 zested and juiced for the marinade, 1 quartered to serve

content.text: Eight wooden or metal skewers; soak wood ones for thirty minutes before threading. A heavy griddle pan or a barbecue with the coals white.

Combine the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, the zest and juice of the {{lemon}} (one of them), the {{garlic}} of grated garlic, the {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper in a medium bowl. Add the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thigh pieces and turn to coat. Cover and refrigerate for two hours, or up to twenty-four. Bring the bowl back to room temperature for the last twenty minutes before cooking; the meat cooks more evenly if it is not fridge-cold.

Thread the chicken cubes onto skewers, leaving a small gap between each piece so the heat reaches the sides. Wipe a griddle pan with a film of oil and heat it over high heat until it is smoking hard. Lay the skewers across the ridges (or onto the barbecue grill) and do not move them for three minutes. Turn a quarter turn; cook three minutes more. Repeat on the remaining two faces. The total is twelve minutes; the surface should be charred in stripes and the centres should be just cooked through. Rest the skewers on a warm plate for five minutes.

Warm the {{pitta-bread}} pittas on the griddle for thirty seconds a side, or in the oven at 200°C for two minutes. Lay a pitta on each plate. Slide the chicken off a skewer into the pitta, scatter over the {{tomato}} sliced tomatoes, the {{red-onion}} sliced red onion, and the {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Finish with a squeeze of the second {{lemon}}. Tzatziki goes on the table in a bowl, to spoon over as you eat.

items.symptom: Chicken stuck to the griddle when turning
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or moved too soon.
items.fix: Heat the griddle until it is faintly smoking before the meat goes on. Do not try to turn for the first three minutes; the meat releases when it is ready.

items.symptom: Outside burnt before the centre cooked
items.cause: Pieces too large, or heat too high.
items.fix: Cut to 3 cm cubes, no bigger. If the surface is browning fast on a barbecue with high heat, move the skewers to a cooler part of the grill to finish.

items.symptom: Texture is mealy and slightly chalky
items.cause: Marinated too long; the lemon acid denatured the protein.
items.fix: Twenty-four hours is the ceiling. If a longer wait is unavoidable, marinate with no lemon juice and add the lemon to the rest of the marinade only in the last two hours.

items.cause: Breast used instead of thigh, or cooked too long.
items.fix: Switch to thigh. If breast is what you have, pull the skewers at ten minutes total and let them rest; breast finishes cooking off the heat.

items.cause: Pitta was over-warmed and went brittle, or filling overstuffed.
items.fix: Warm the pitta only until pliable, around thirty seconds a side. Do not warm so far that it puffs and crisps; that is for a different dish.

content.text: Pork souvlaki uses cubes of pork shoulder in the same marinade; the cooking time is the same. Lamb shoulder is the third option, an island variation. For a more substantial wrap (gyros-style pita), add a handful of chips inside the pitta. To bulk for a crowd, serve the skewers off the bread with rice or roast potatoes, a Greek salad alongside, and tzatziki and lemon on the table.

Souvlaki is the everyday Greek grill dish, sold from carts and tavernas across Greece and exported intact to every Greek diaspora kitchen. The pita-wrapped version is the street form; the plate version is the sit-down restaurant order. The recipe holds across both. What changes between cooks is small: how long to marinate, whether to add bay or thyme, whether to use lemon juice or vinegar. The skewer and the oregano are the constants.
